title: Retinal Detachment
oneLiner: Painless separation of retina from RPE causing vision loss; urgent ophthalmology emergency; permanent blindness if macula detaches.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute angle closure glaucoma:** Rock-hard globe (IOP >40), cloudy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, severe pain + nausea. Do NOT dilate pupil; measure IOP immediately.
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA):** Unilateral sudden vision loss in elderly (>50) with headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, elevated ESR/CRP. **DO NOT DELAY** — start prednisolone 60 mg PO stat (after urgent ophthalmology +/- temporal artery biopsy) to protect contralateral eye.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Sudden onset of floaters ("cobwebs" or "flies"), photopsia (flashing lights in peripheral vision, especially in dim light), then a dark "curtain" or "veil" that progresses centrally. Painless vision loss (unless associated with trauma or inflammation).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced visual acuity (varies with location — macula-on may have good central vision initially)
  - Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) present if detachment large enough to cause a visual field defect
  - Visual field defect on confrontation testing (diminished red perception or complete loss in that quadrant)
  - Ophthalmoscopy (dilated fundus exam by senior or ophthalmologist): pale, elevated retina with folds, visible retinal tear(s) (often horse-shoe shaped), and vitreous hemorrhage ("tobacco dust" = pigment granules in anterior vitreous)
  - Intraocular pressure usually normal (unless concurrent glaucoma)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) without retinal tear:** Common benign cause of floaters/flashes; no visual field defect, no RAPD, normal retina on dilated exam. **Splitter:** Presence of a visual field defect or RAPD points to retinal detachment (or tear). In PVD, symptoms often resolve over weeks; no curtain.
- **Vitreous haemorrhage:** Can mimic detachment; no obvious retinal elevation on ultrasound; look for underlying retinal tear (do B-scan). Splitter: B-scan shows blood in vitreous cavity without retinal detachment.
- **Optic neuritis:** Younger patient, pain on eye movement, central scotoma, dyschromatopsia, normal retina. No RAPD? Actually RAPD is typical. But no floaters.
- **Migraine / migraine aura:** Positive visual phenomena (zigzags, scotoma) last <1 hour, followed by headache; no persistent field defect, normal fundus exam.
- **Retinal artery occlusion (CRAO/ BRAO):** Sudden painless vision loss, cherry-red spot, attenuated arterioles; no curtain/floaters. Requires immediate IOP lowering and stroke workup.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Visual acuity (Snellen chart if available, otherwise counting fingers/hand motion), pupillary exam (RAPD), confrontation visual fields, Amsler grid if able, otoscopy (to clear visual axis), and measurement of intraocular pressure (only if glaucoma suspected — but do not dilate if IOP elevated).
- **Bloods:** FBC, ESR, CRP (if GCA suspected). Otherwise no routine bloods for isolated retinal detachment.
- **Imaging:** First-line — **dilated fundoscopy** by ophthalmologist. Second-line — **B-scan ultrasound** if media opacity (e.g., vitreous hemorrhage, cataract) prevents adequate view; can confirm retinal detachment and distinguish from vitreous hemorrhage alone.
- **Special tests:** None routinely performed by FY1. Fundus photography may be done by ophthalmology.
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent ophthalmology consult** — this is the definitive diagnostic test. Do not delay for any other investigation. Also check **IOP** to exclude acute glaucoma.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Urgent ophthalmology referral (same-day emergency). Call the on-call ophthalmology registrar directly.
  - If suspect GCA, **do not wait** — start prednisolone 60 mg PO (or IV if severe) and refer to ophthalmology for biopsy.
  - Analgesia if painful (but pain is rare in retinal detachment; if severe pain, consider acute glaucoma).
  - Position patient? No evidence for routine positioning before surgery; do not give specific head position instructions (this is decided by ophthalmology based on detachment location).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If admitted for surgery: keep patient NBM after midnight if surgery planned next day. Maintain head position as per ophthalmology (e.g., face-down for gas tamponade).
  - IV fluids if needed for hydration (usually not required).
  - Eye shield if post-laser or surgery (ophthalmology will prescribe).
  - No eye patching — this delays assessment and can worsen glaucoma if unrecognised.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Surgical repair within 24–72 hours if macula-on (to preserve central vision). If macula-off, urgent but can wait up to 7 days for best outcome.
  - Procedures: vitrectomy with gas/silicone oil tamponade, scleral buckle, or laser photocoagulation.
  - Post-op: strict head positioning, avoid straining, no heavy lifting, no air travel (if gas bubble), and use of topical antibiotics + steroids as per ophthalmology.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline at 1 mL/kg/h if NBM (otherwise no routine fluids).
  - VTE prophylaxis: Assess risk (caprini score) but generally low risk in this setting; only if prolonged immobility.
  - Diet/NBM: if surgery planned within 24h, keep NBM from midnight. If surgery not immediate, allow free fluids.
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 4 hours if stable. If GCA suspected, monitor blood pressure and blood glucose (due to steroids).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **No drug therapy exists to treat retinal detachment itself.**
- **For symptomatic posterior vitreous detachment** (if no tear): no specific medication; lubricating drops if corneal irritation.
- **If GCA is suspected (before ophthalmology review):**
  - Prednisolone 60 mg PO once daily (or IV methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg if severe vision loss — [check local policy])
- **Topical mydriatics for examination (by ophthalmologist only):**
  - Cyclopentolate 1% + phenylephrine 2.5% (both 1 drop every 10–15 minutes up to 3 times). **Do NOT administer if IOP elevated (undiagnosed glaucoma).**
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** None specific. Avoid NSAIDs if diabetic retinopathy with vitreous haemorrhage (theoretical risk of bleeding).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prednisolone in undiagnosed infectious uveitis** — but acute retinal detachment is not typical for uveitis. More relevant: **overdose of topical anaesthetics (proxymetacaine) causing corneal toxicity** — do not use for repeated exams.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Permanent vision loss (especially if macula detached >72h).  
  - Proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR) — scar tissue formation causing recurrent detachment.  
  - Elevated IOP (glaucoma) secondary to inflammation or silicone oil.  
  - Endophthalmitis (post-operative, rare but serious).  
  - Vitreous haemorrhage (can obscure view and delay surgery).
- **Chronic:**  
  - Macular pucker (epiretinal membrane) – distortion/ reduced vision.  
  - Chronic retinal detachment (if untreated – leads to phthisis bulbi).  
  - Bilateral involvement in some conditions (e.g., high myopia, Stickler syndrome).
sections.Prognosis:
- **If macula-on** and repaired within 24–72 hours: >90% maintain 6/12 vision or better.  
- **If macula-off:** only 40-60% regain useful vision (6/12–6/60) even after successful reattachment.  
- Delay >7 days significantly worsens outcome.  
- Recurrence rate: 5-10% (higher with PVR or large tears).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check for RAPD** in any patient with sudden vision loss — it’s the fastest way to identify a large detachment.  
- **Perform confrontation visual fields in all four quadrants** using a red pin — a relative scotoma is an early sign.  
- **If a patient describes a "curtain" that moves, take it seriously** — even if visual acuity is normal (macula-on). Call ophthalmology immediately.  
- **Document exact time of symptom onset** — this determines urgency of surgery (macula-off time).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT dilate the pupil if you suspect acute angle closure glaucoma** (shallow anterior chamber, IOP high). This can precipitate an attack.  
- **Do NOT discharge a patient with new floaters/flashes without a dilated fundus exam** by a trained clinician — missed tears can progress to detachment within hours.  
- **Do NOT use eye patching** in suspected detachment — it prevents both assessment and treatment, and can mask worsening glaucoma.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New onset of curtain/veil in any visual field (even if previously diagnosed as PVD), sudden increase in number of floaters, new flashes of light, decreased vision, or redness/pain (contraindication to gas).  
- **Follow-up:** If diagnosed as PVD without tear, arrange outpatient review by ophthalmology in 2–6 weeks (if symptomatic) or sooner if symptoms worsen. Patients with high-risk features (high myopia, lattice degeneration, family history) should see an ophthalmologist within 1 week.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ophthalmology senior (reg or consultant):**  
  - Suspected retinal detachment with RAPD (especially if macula-on).  
  - Any sudden vision loss with pain (exclude acute glaucoma).  
  - Post-operative patient with decreased vision, severe pain, or discharge (endophthalmitis).  
- **Call crash team:** Not applicable — retinal detachment does not cause cardiovascular compromise.  
- **Specific thresholds:** None in terms of NEWS2. However, if patient has GCA and develops new visual loss, this is a **vision-threatening emergency** — escalate to ophthalmology regardless of vital signs.

rawMarkdown:
## Retinal Detachment

**One-liner:** Painless separation of retina from RPE causing vision loss; urgent ophthalmology emergency; permanent blindness if macula detaches.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute angle closure glaucoma:** Rock-hard globe (IOP >40), cloudy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, severe pain + nausea. Do NOT dilate pupil; measure IOP immediately.
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA):** Unilateral sudden vision loss in elderly (>50) with headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, elevated ESR/CRP. **DO NOT DELAY** — start prednisolone 60 mg PO stat (after urgent ophthalmology +/- temporal artery biopsy) to protect contralateral eye.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Sudden onset of floaters ("cobwebs" or "flies"), photopsia (flashing lights in peripheral vision, especially in dim light), then a dark "curtain" or "veil" that progresses centrally. Painless vision loss (unless associated with trauma or inflammation).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced visual acuity (varies with location — macula-on may have good central vision initially)
  - Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) present if detachment large enough to cause a visual field defect
  - Visual field defect on confrontation testing (diminished red perception or complete loss in that quadrant)
  - Ophthalmoscopy (dilated fundus exam by senior or ophthalmologist): pale, elevated retina with folds, visible retinal tear(s) (often horse-shoe shaped), and vitreous hemorrhage ("tobacco dust" = pigment granules in anterior vitreous)
  - Intraocular pressure usually normal (unless concurrent glaucoma)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) without retinal tear:** Common benign cause of floaters/flashes; no visual field defect, no RAPD, normal retina on dilated exam. **Splitter:** Presence of a visual field defect or RAPD points to retinal detachment (or tear). In PVD, symptoms often resolve over weeks; no curtain.
- **Vitreous haemorrhage:** Can mimic detachment; no obvious retinal elevation on ultrasound; look for underlying retinal tear (do B-scan). Splitter: B-scan shows blood in vitreous cavity without retinal detachment.
- **Optic neuritis:** Younger patient, pain on eye movement, central scotoma, dyschromatopsia, normal retina. No RAPD? Actually RAPD is typical. But no floaters.
- **Migraine / migraine aura:** Positive visual phenomena (zigzags, scotoma) last <1 hour, followed by headache; no persistent field defect, normal fundus exam.
- **Retinal artery occlusion (CRAO/ BRAO):** Sudden painless vision loss, cherry-red spot, attenuated arterioles; no curtain/floaters. Requires immediate IOP lowering and stroke workup.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Visual acuity (Snellen chart if available, otherwise counting fingers/hand motion), pupillary exam (RAPD), confrontation visual fields, Amsler grid if able, otoscopy (to clear visual axis), and measurement of intraocular pressure (only if glaucoma suspected — but do not dilate if IOP elevated).
- **Bloods:** FBC, ESR, CRP (if GCA suspected). Otherwise no routine bloods for isolated retinal detachment.
- **Imaging:** First-line — **dilated fundoscopy** by ophthalmologist. Second-line — **B-scan ultrasound** if media opacity (e.g., vitreous hemorrhage, cataract) prevents adequate view; can confirm retinal detachment and distinguish from vitreous hemorrhage alone.
- **Special tests:** None routinely performed by FY1. Fundus photography may be done by ophthalmology.
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent ophthalmology consult** — this is the definitive diagnostic test. Do not delay for any other investigation. Also check **IOP** to exclude acute glaucoma.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Urgent ophthalmology referral (same-day emergency). Call the on-call ophthalmology registrar directly.
  - If suspect GCA, **do not wait** — start prednisolone 60 mg PO (or IV if severe) and refer to ophthalmology for biopsy.
  - Analgesia if painful (but pain is rare in retinal detachment; if severe pain, consider acute glaucoma).
  - Position patient? No evidence for routine positioning before surgery; do not give specific head position instructions (this is decided by ophthalmology based on detachment location).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If admitted for surgery: keep patient NBM after midnight if surgery planned next day. Maintain head position as per ophthalmology (e.g., face-down for gas tamponade).
  - IV fluids if needed for hydration (usually not required).
  - Eye shield if post-laser or surgery (ophthalmology will prescribe).
  - No eye patching — this delays assessment and can worsen glaucoma if unrecognised.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Surgical repair within 24–72 hours if macula-on (to preserve central vision). If macula-off, urgent but can wait up to 7 days for best outcome.
  - Procedures: vitrectomy with gas/silicone oil tamponade, scleral buckle, or laser photocoagulation.
  - Post-op: strict head positioning, avoid straining, no heavy lifting, no air travel (if gas bubble), and use of topical antibiotics + steroids as per ophthalmology.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline at 1 mL/kg/h if NBM (otherwise no routine fluids).
  - VTE prophylaxis: Assess risk (caprini score) but generally low risk in this setting; only if prolonged immobility.
  - Diet/NBM: if surgery planned within 24h, keep NBM from midnight. If surgery not immediate, allow free fluids.
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 4 hours if stable. If GCA suspected, monitor blood pressure and blood glucose (due to steroids).

### Drugs & Doses
- **No drug therapy exists to treat retinal detachment itself.**
- **For symptomatic posterior vitreous detachment** (if no tear): no specific medication; lubricating drops if corneal irritation.
- **If GCA is suspected (before ophthalmology review):**
  - Prednisolone 60 mg PO once daily (or IV methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg if severe vision loss — [check local policy])
- **Topical mydriatics for examination (by ophthalmologist only):**
  - Cyclopentolate 1% + phenylephrine 2.5% (both 1 drop every 10–15 minutes up to 3 times). **Do NOT administer if IOP elevated (undiagnosed glaucoma).**
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** None specific. Avoid NSAIDs if diabetic retinopathy with vitreous haemorrhage (theoretical risk of bleeding).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prednisolone in undiagnosed infectious uveitis** — but acute retinal detachment is not typical for uveitis. More relevant: **overdose of topical anaesthetics (proxymetacaine) causing corneal toxicity** — do not use for repeated exams.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Permanent vision loss (especially if macula detached >72h).  
  - Proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR) — scar tissue formation causing recurrent detachment.  
  - Elevated IOP (glaucoma) secondary to inflammation or silicone oil.  
  - Endophthalmitis (post-operative, rare but serious).  
  - Vitreous haemorrhage (can obscure view and delay surgery).
- **Chronic:**  
  - Macular pucker (epiretinal membrane) – distortion/ reduced vision.  
  - Chronic retinal detachment (if untreated – leads to phthisis bulbi).  
  - Bilateral involvement in some conditions (e.g., high myopia, Stickler syndrome).

### Prognosis
- **If macula-on** and repaired within 24–72 hours: >90% maintain 6/12 vision or better.  
- **If macula-off:** only 40-60% regain useful vision (6/12–6/60) even after successful reattachment.  
- Delay >7 days significantly worsens outcome.  
- Recurrence rate: 5-10% (higher with PVR or large tears).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check for RAPD** in any patient with sudden vision loss — it’s the fastest way to identify a large detachment.  
- **Perform confrontation visual fields in all four quadrants** using a red pin — a relative scotoma is an early sign.  
- **If a patient describes a "curtain" that moves, take it seriously** — even if visual acuity is normal (macula-on). Call ophthalmology immediately.  
- **Document exact time of symptom onset** — this determines urgency of surgery (macula-off time).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT dilate the pupil if you suspect acute angle closure glaucoma** (shallow anterior chamber, IOP high). This can precipitate an attack.  
- **Do NOT discharge a patient with new floaters/flashes without a dilated fundus exam** by a trained clinician — missed tears can progress to detachment within hours.  
- **Do NOT use eye patching** in suspected detachment — it prevents both assessment and treatment, and can mask worsening glaucoma.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New onset of curtain/veil in any visual field (even if previously diagnosed as PVD), sudden increase in number of floaters, new flashes of light, decreased vision, or redness/pain (contraindication to gas).  
- **Follow-up:** If diagnosed as PVD without tear, arrange outpatient review by ophthalmology in 2–6 weeks (if symptomatic) or sooner if symptoms worsen. Patients with high-risk features (high myopia, lattice degeneration, family history) should see an ophthalmologist within 1 week.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call ophthalmology senior (reg or consultant):**  
  - Suspected retinal detachment with RAPD (especially if macula-on).  
  - Any sudden vision loss with pain (exclude acute glaucoma).  
  - Post-operative patient with decreased vision, severe pain, or discharge (endophthalmitis).  
- **Call crash team:** Not applicable — retinal detachment does not cause cardiovascular compromise.  
- **Specific thresholds:** None in terms of NEWS2. However, if patient has GCA and develops new visual loss, this is a **vision-threatening emergency** — escalate to ophthalmology regardless of vital signs.
